serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 ml (350 g), equivalent to a regular-sized cup of Yakult green tea as typically served at Singaporean bubble tea or specialty tea stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Yakult Green Tea (No Topping) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Yakult green tea (no topping), cr_id D08010098. All per-100g values are taken as reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Yakult green tea. GI estimated at 55 based on the analogous food 'sugar-sweetened tea beverage' from the University of Sydney GI Database. The beverage is predominantly sugar-sweetened (7.49g sugar/100g, all carbohydrate as sugar) with negligible fibre. A GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (≤55) per the University of Sydney classification scale. Glycemic load per 350g serving = (55 × 7.49 × 350) / (100 × 100) = 14.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (7.49g sugar/100g) exceeding the 5g/100g beverage sugar threshold; sodium is low at 1mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is very low (1mg/100g), the high sugar content (7.49g/100g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage that independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines; individuals with hypertension should choose plain unsweetened green tea instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.49g sugar/100g and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar raises postprandial glucose rapidly
type2_diabetes.guidance: This beverage contains 7.49g of sugar per 100g with no dietary fibre, classifying it as a sugar-sweetened beverage that causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this drink and choose plain unsweetened green tea per ADA guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar content (7.49g/100g) with zero saturated fat; regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased CVD risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sugar load (7.49g/100g, all carbohydrate as sugar) with zero saturated fat and very low sodium presents a moderate CVD concern driven by sugar-sweetened beverage consumption patterns; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this drink and opt for unsweetened green tea, which provides cardioprotective polyphenols without the sugar burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar at 7.49g/100g with zero fibre promotes pro-inflammatory mediators that outweigh the anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefit of the diluted green tea component
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The pro-inflammatory sugar load (7.49g/100g) in this beverage outweighs the anti-inflammatory EGCG polyphenol benefit of the diluted green tea; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request no added sugar or choose plain unsweetened green tea.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yakult Green Tea (No Topping) is a popular Singaporean specialty tea beverage made by blending freshly brewed green tea with Yakult, a Japanese probiotic fermented milk drink. The resulting beverage has a light, mildly sweet, and slightly tangy flavour profile, with the grassy, vegetal notes of green tea balanced by the characteristic lactic tartness of Yakult. It is served chilled and is widely available at bubble tea shops and specialty tea stalls across Singapore. The 'no topping' variant omits customisable additions such as pearls, jelly, or pudding, resulting in a lower-calorie, lower-carbohydrate version compared to the standard topped offering. Per 100g, the beverage provides 29 kcal, 7.49g carbohydrate (entirely as sugar), 0.01g protein, 0g fat, 0g dietary fibre, and 1mg sodium. Clinically, the beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to its 7.49g sugar per 100g content with zero fibre, making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The Yakult component contributes Lactobacillus casei Shirota probiotic bacteria and trace calcium and iron. Plain unsweetened green tea is recommended as a healthier alternative for individuals with metabolic or cardiovascular conditions.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010098) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and canonical. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from an analogous approved source (University of Sydney GI Database) with documented basis.
notes: fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database; this is a tea-based beverage with negligible fat content by formulation. saturated_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database consistent with the fat_g value. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database; brewed tea beverages contain no measurable dietary fibre. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this specific Yakult green tea formulation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry as of 2026. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on sugar-sweetened tea beverage from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Yakult green tea was found in approved sources. GI 55 is classified as low per the University of Sydney GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). The Yakult component contains skimmed milk, making this beverage non-vegan and non-dairy-free. Halal status is based on Yakult Singapore being Halal-certified by MUIS.

Iteration 2: Verified gi_category against GI value; previous draft incorrectly set gi_category to 'medium' for GI 55.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI 55 is the upper boundary of the low GI category per University of Sydney classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Updated gi_source notes and record notes to document this classification basis explicitly.
